zh.lifeSummary: Lady John Lucas I於1395年出生在英國肯特。她和John Lucas I至少有1個兒子。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: Lady John Lucas I于1395年出生在英国肯特。她和John Lucas I至少有1个儿子。

nameMeanings.name: Lucas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English, German, French, Spanish, Portuguese, and Dutch: from the Latin personal name Lucas (Greek Loukas) ‘man from Lucania’. Lucania is a region of southern Italy thought to have been named in ancient times with a word meaning ‘bright’ or ‘shining’ (compare Lucio ). The Christian name owed its enormous popularity throughout Europe in the Middle Ages to Saint Luke the Evangelist, hence the development of this surname and many vernacular derivatives in most of the languages of Europe. In North America, this surname has absorbed cognates from other languages, e.g. Greek Loukas , Hungarian Lukács (see Lukacs ), German, Dutch, etc. Lukas , Polish Łukasz, Czech and Slovak Lukáš, Czech Lukeš, Slovenian and Croatian Lukež (see Lukes ). Compare Luke .
Scottish: shortened Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Lùcais (see McLucas ).
History: Jacques Lucas dit Lépine from Port-en-Bessin-Huppain in Calvados, France, married Françoise Capel in Trois-Rivières, QC, in 1653.

variants.name: Lekan
meanings: Slovenian: from a vernacular form of the old personal name Lukan, derived from Luka (see Lucas ).
Polish: unexplained.

variants.name: Lockett
meanings: English (northern): from a pet form of   Lucas  .

variants.name: Luca
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Rocco, Adolfo, Antonio, Carmine, Gasper, Giacomo, Mario, Rosario, Sal, Aldo, Antonino, Domenic, Domenico, Dominico, Guido.
Italian and Romanian: from the personal name Luca, from Latin Lucas . Compare Louque .
Italian (southern; Lucà): possibly from a short form of Greek Loukanos, denoting an inhabitant of Lucania (see Lucas ).

variants.name: Lauck
meanings: German (Hesse): from the personal name Lukas (see Lucas ), or from the female personal name Liutgard (see Luckhardt ).
Germanized form of Sorbian Ławk and Lawk: from a pet form of the personal name Ławŕeńc, Lawŕeńc, from Latin Laurentius (see Lawrence ).

variants.name: Lockey
meanings: English: habitational name from Locka in Melling (Lancashire). The placename derives from Old English loc ‘lock, enclosure, fold’ + haga ‘hedge, enclosure’.
Scottish: from a pet form of   Lucas  . Compare   Lockie  .

variants.name: Lucio
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Juan, Luis, Francisco, Jesus, Manuel, Pedro, Angel, Enrique, Felipe, Fernando, Guadalupe.
Spanish (also Lucío), Portuguese (Lúcio), and Italian: from local equivalents of the Latin personal name Lucius .
Galician: habitational name from Lucio in Pontevedra province, Galicia (Spain).

variants.name: Loukas
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Greek Spero, Christos, Dimitrios, Gerasimos, Kostas, Loukas, Pantelis.
Greek: from the personal name Loukas, Greek form of Lucas .

variants.name: Luc
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Vietnamese Anh, Minh, Mui, Duong, Hong, Hung, Nu, Thanh, Duc, Dung, Hoa, Huu, Long, Hang, Heung, Tong.  French Pierre, Jean, Gabrielle, Germain, Sylvain.
French: from the personal name Luc, a vernacular form of Lucas .
French: topographic name derived from Latin lucus ‘wood’, or a habitational name from any of the places in Aveyron and Lozère named with this word.

variants.name: Laux
meanings: South German: from a vernacular form of the personal name   Lucas  . Compare   Loucks  .

variants.name: Lockie
meanings: Scottish:
perhaps from the Scottish personal name Lockie, Luckie, a pet form of Lucas .
variant of   Lockey   1.

nameMeanings.name: John
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament: ‘God is gracious’. See John .

historicEvents.title: Wars of the Roses
historicEvents.description: Wars of the Roses was a series of English civil wars for control of the throne of England between the royal families House of Lancaster and the House of York. The name of the war was derived as the red rose was the symbol of the Lancastrians and the white rose was the symbol of the Yorks.
